title: The NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 system is 86-88% cheaper than the A100 on a like-for-like performance basis. | Ben Baldieri
text:
The NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 system is 86-88% cheaper than the A100 on a like-for-like performance basis. What does this mean for AI labs, GPU providers, and data centre operators? The NVL72 system is finally hitting the market at scale. And there's no comparison in performance. Pete Overell and I have been hearing about some absolutely ridiculous performance benchmarks from partners. A single GB200 compute tray (4 Blackwell GPUs and 2 Grace CPUs from Arm) seems to be as performant as ~64 A100 GPUs, or 8 A100 nodes. There are 18 compute trays in one NVL72 rack. That means a single rack delivers the same performance as ~144 A100 servers. What about costs? At launch, an A100 system was ~$200k. As of right now, an NVL72 system is $3.5-4m. That means for equivalent performance, you would need to spend: = 144 x $200k = $28,800,000 That means the NVL72 system is 86-88% cheaper on a like-for-like performance basis. What about power consumption differences? An NVL72 rack draws a rated max of ~135kW. One A100 system draws a rated max of ~6.5kW. Therefore, for an equivalent level of performance: = 144 x 6.5kW = 936kW What about physical space? The NVL72 system is a rack-level solution, so it's 1 rack, so we can conservatively assume a physical footprint of ~2m x 1m, or 2m^2. For the A100, if we take the average European rack density of 8kW, you'll need 144 racks. Assuming the same rack footprint, that's ~288m^2. If you're a GPU provider, therefore, and you wanted to deploy A100 systems instead of GB200s, you'd have to: • Account for 7.2x - 8.22x more CapEx • Factor 6.9x more power cost into your OpEx And if you're a data centre operator looking to attract GPU providers: • You'll need to supply 6.9x more power • With 144x more floor space This is, obviously, an extreme example. Assumptions have been made. Efficiencies could be gained. The performance jump, however, makes one thing clear: If you're a GPU provider or data centre operator and you're still of the mind that the market doesn't want these super high-powered, high-density systems, take another look at your thesis. These systems are here today. The GB300s will be even more performant. The Vera-Rubin systems will be even more performant again. And the numbers don't lie. If you're looking to deploy, support, or access GB200s, we can help: • We're helping several partners bring NVL72 systems online right now • We're deploying into European GB200-ready data centres • These systems will be some of the first in Europe And that's not all: • We can bring finance partners in to support • We can help you upgrade your data centre • We can find you a GB200-ready facility • We can build you the financial models • We can build the business case • We can procure the systems • We can find you an offtake The way I see it, you have 2 choices: Adapt. Or get left behind. Shoot one of the Panchaea Ltd team a message, and let's get started.

      My take on GPUs having a real 1-2 year life usefulness instead of +4 years is opening up many questions, so let me explain in more detail: First, all of the counter arguments are the following "but H100, A100 are still being used and they are 3-5 years old", "customers will use old GPUs for inference workloads", "big tech is using old GPUs for internal workloads" Why this is the wrong thinking: 1. People forget that $NVDA has gone to a 1-year product cycle in 2024 (not sooner!), so Blackwell is still the product of a 2-year product cycle. Before Blackwell Hopper -H100, H200 was the product, and before that, the A100 was the product. So first of all, H100 is not a 3-year-old product; it is a 1-year-old product in terms of product cycle (just after Blackwell). Nvidia also started shipping the H100 with serious volumes at the start of 2023. Going forward from today, every year we will get a new product which will be significantly more performant (10x-30x) and efficient than the previous generation, so not every 2 years but every year. 2. We are transitioning from a world where every additional GPU/accelerator deployment is incremental to a world where most of the deployment is replacement (not incremental), as we are limited. We are limited by power and available data centers. So, of course, when you have an abundance of data center space & power, you are also going to be using the "old" GPUs, as you have enough space to deploy them. But once you run out of that deployment space and are limited, your limiting factor is power, and so you are looking for how many tokens do you generate per watt that you have. If the new generation of GPUs gives you 10x the tokens/watt than the previous one, if you want to grow and serve more clients, you will have to replace it with the new generation, and you can't "use" the "old" GPU, as you don't have any place to deploy it. Again, the thing to understand is that we are moving from GPU scarce to POWER scarce, and this changes things. 3. To paint a picture of "oh, but the old GPUs will be used for internal workloads" is wrong. There is only a handful of companies that have the luxury of having their own cloud business, plus having also a big consumer or enterprise business, which can take on some of these older GPUs (again, even these use-cases will drastically be reduced as we enter the phase described in argument 2). The cloud providers won't have enough demand and good margins to run the "old" generation of GPUs for cloud customers, as these GPUs are not assets that just make money once you buy them; they also cost. They cost power (electricity prices are rising), they cost in cooling, and they cost in maintenance. 4. Inference with thinking and reasoning models has changed drastically. Now, a smaller, less performant model in terms of parameters can perform better than a bigger model, if you give it more compute on the inference side "to think". To view or add a comment, sign in

Excellent review…. But one key consideration seems to be missing: the impact of hardware depreciation. Valuing a GB200 investment across a 60-month term is highly optimistic given the current pace of innovation. For example, a single NVIDIA A100 retailed for around $25,000 in late 2021. Today, just 3.5 years later, that same card is worth approximately 75% to 85% less and with newer, faster architectures emerging at an accelerated rate, we can expect even steeper declines moving forward. The lesson here. Building a business plan on the assumption of a five-year lifecycle for leading-edge hardware is risky. Depreciation and technological obsolescence are accelerating. To achieve sustainable success, financial models must be aligned with a much shorter return-on-investment horizon.
